explanation: subway brakes. Usually a piercing sound.

discriminators: UCS says it outright: 'Usually a piercing sound.' A brake squeal is a STICK-SLIP friction oscillation — an unstable, WANDERING, INHARMONIC high tone. So it is by far the brightest thing in TRAINS (spectral_centroid_hz ~3.8 kHz, high_band_energy ~0.55) while carrying enough harmonicity to actually squeal, with high inharmonicity and a large spectral_centroid_deviation_hz because the pitch will not sit still.
Against every rolling and engine subcategory (TRAINS-DIESEL, TRAINS-ELECTRIC, TRAINS-CLACK): the spectral tilt is INVERTED. Those are LF-dominant; this is HF-dominant. That is a real separation.
Against TRAINS-HORN: both are loud and pitched, but a horn is STABLE, HARMONIC and LOW (~300 Hz), while a squeal is unstable, inharmonic and in the kilohertz. No overlap.
Narrowable, not separable, because metal-on-metal screech is acoustically the same wherever it comes from — METAL-FRICTION or VEHICLES-BRAKE will look identical.

explanation: constant clacking of trains passing or running.

discriminators: The textbook rhythmic detector, and one of the few places where a rhythm detector alone is nearly sufficient. Wheels crossing rail joints produce a PERIODIC IMPULSE TRAIN whose rate is set by bogie spacing and train speed — a few clacks per second, each a sharp metallic transient with a short decay. onset_periodicity is very high (~0.80, gated >= 0.35), onset_rate_per_second sits in the 3-8/s band, crest_factor is high (~7) because the signal is mostly gaps, and envelope_attack_seconds is a few milliseconds.
Nothing else in TRAINS is BOTH impulsive AND strictly periodic at that rate.
Against TRAINS-STEAM: chuffs are also periodic but SLOWER (~1-3/s), softer-attacked, and ride on a continuous HF hiss bed that clacking lacks.
Against TRAINS-MECHANISM: coupling clanks are ISOLATED impacts, not a sustained periodic train — hence the transient_count >= 4 gate here, which a couple of shunting bangs will not pass.
A decelerating train sweeps the clack rate downward; the periodicity survives the sweep, the rate does not, so the rate prior is given a wide deviation.

explanation: sliding doors in passenger or freight trains. Some overlap with general DOORS category and possibly VEHICLES-DOOR.

discriminators: A sliding subway door is a pneumatic hiss plus a hard end-stop clunk — but so is a bus door, and UCS itself flags the overlap with the general DOORS category and VEHICLES-DOOR. Semantic.
The gates still earn their keep: an event of seconds, not a rolling bed, which prunes it against TRAINS-INTERIOR, TRAINS-SUBWAY and the locomotive subcategories.

A big diesel prime mover: LOW, LOUD, DISTORTED, with an exhaust firing pulse train. low_band_energy ~0.60, heavy total_harmonic_distortion, moderate-to-high onset_periodicity at tens of pulses per second.

Against TRAINS-ELECTRIC: this is the one intra-TRAINS engine boundary the signal can actually see, and it is worth stating precisely. A traction motor is a clean tonal WHINE — high harmonicity, low spectral_flatness, a centroid an octave or more higher, near-zero combustion periodicity, and a pitch that GLIDES with speed. A diesel is noisy, LF, distorted and pulse-periodic. Genuinely narrowable.

Against TRAINS-CLACK: a clack is a sparse impulsive train (a few per second, crest_factor ~7); the diesel is a continuous bed (crest_factor ~4). Both often appear in one recording — prefer whichever dominates the energy.
Freight versus passenger diesel is semantic.

A traction motor and its inverter make a TONE, not a rumble: harmonicity ~0.52, spectral_flatness down at ~0.28, and a measurable pitch_hz in the hundreds of hertz that GLIDES monotonically with train speed (pitch_slope_semitones_per_second nonzero) — the characteristic accelerating whine. And, decisively, no combustion pulse train: onset_periodicity ~0.20.
That pairing — TONAL plus NON-PULSED — is precisely what TRAINS-DIESEL is not (noisy, LF, distorted, pulse-periodic). It is the real signal boundary inside this category.

Against TRAINS-SUBWAY: acoustically the SAME electric traction. UCS separates them by location ('overtly underground sounding'), which surfaces as reverberation, not as a source feature — semantic, with only a weak tunnel-reverb tell.
UCS routes cable-hauled trams to TRAINS-TRAM and electric trams here.

At speed, aerodynamic noise swamps everything: the wheel clack blurs away (onset_periodicity falls) and what is left is a brief, bright, broadband WHOOSH. There is a real physical reason the rhythmic signature is ABSENT here — high-speed lines use continuously welded rail, so the joints that make a clack do not exist.

A high-speed pass-by carries a strong DOPPLER: spectral_centroid_slope_hz_per_second passes through zero with a rise-fall envelope. That is measurable, but it identifies a PASS-BY, not a bullet train.
Which is why this is semantic_only: a TGV, a Shinkansen and a fast conventional express are the same aerodynamic roar. The synonyms (Shinkansen, TGV, Maglev, Hyperloop) carry it.

explanation: subway horns. A steam train whistle would likely go into WHISTLES-MECHANICAL.

discriminators: Loud, low, stable, harmonic — and HELD. A locomotive horn is a multi-chime air horn, so it sounds a fixed CHORD: harmonicity ~0.70 (gated >= 0.35), spectral_flatness ~0.16, a high partial_count, a flat pitch_slope_semitones_per_second, and a plateau envelope with a high sustain_ratio. Fully measurable.
Against every other TRAINS subcategory: they are noise, rhythm or friction; this is the only stable TONE, and the harmonicity gate rejects the rolling stock outright.
Against TRAINS-BRAKE (the other loud, pitched candidate): a squeal is INHARMONIC, UNSTABLE and in the kilohertz; a horn is harmonic, stable and a few hundred hertz. No overlap.
Against ALARMS-SIREN: a siren sweeps its pitch; a horn does not. Against BOATS-HORN: an acoustic near-twin — text decides, though a ship's horn runs lower and longer.
UCS sends steam whistles to WHISTLES-MECHANICAL, so a shrill single-tone whistle is NOT this.
Doppler on a pass-by will bend the pitch; the pitch_slope deviation is set wide enough (2.0 st/s) to survive it.

An LP-filtered rumble bed like every vehicle interior — but with ONE feature the others do not have: the wheel clack comes up through the floor. A train interior is therefore the only INTERIOR in this set that carries a moderate onset_periodicity (~0.45) at a few events per second on top of a stationary LF bed. That is a genuine, if modest, discriminator against AIRCRAFT-INTERIOR (onset_periodicity ~0.12) and BOATS-INTERIOR (~0.18).

Against TRAINS-CLACK (exterior or isolated): there the clack IS the signal — sharp, crest_factor ~7, HF present. Heard through the floor it is muffled and buried in the bed, so crest_factor stays low (~4.2) and band_limit_high_hz is capped.
Diesel versus commuter interior is semantic.

explanation: decoupling and other mechanical sounds relating to trains. Levers
coal tender mechanics that are specific to a train.

discriminators: Couplings, shunting, axles. A coupler slam is a MASSIVE metal impact: very high crest_factor (~9), a millisecond attack, and a long inharmonic ring (decay_time_seconds_60db approaching a second) — the metal-impedance signature at full scale.
But that is generic METAL-IMPACT morphology and nothing in it says 'train'. Semantic, and it will collide with BOATS-MECHANISM, AIRCRAFT-MECHANISM and the whole METAL category.
Against TRAINS-CLACK: isolated impacts, not a sustained periodic train. CLACK gates at transient_count >= 4 with onset_periodicity >= 0.35; a couple of shunting bangs will not pass it.

explanation: Train sounds not fitting another category in this list.

discriminators: The abstention target for TRAINS. Near-open gates and flat priors by design: it should win when the file is train-shaped but no sibling's morphology gate is met — level crossings, station announcements, yard ambience.

A steam locomotive is TWO signatures at once, and both are measurable. (1) HISS: a broadband HF noise bed — spectral_flatness ~0.58, high_band_energy ~0.40, high zero_crossings_per_second — the AIR-HISS timbre exactly. (2) CHUFF: a slow RHYTHMIC exhaust beat on top — onset_periodicity ~0.68 at only ~1-5 events per second, because there are four exhaust strokes per driver revolution and the drivers turn slowly. HISS + SLOW-PERIODIC is the fingerprint, and it belongs to nothing else in TRAINS.
Against TRAINS-DIESEL: a diesel is LF, distorted and pulses at TENS of hertz; steam is HF-bright, undistorted and pulses at a FEW per second. Both the spectral tilt and the onset rate are inverted.
Against TRAINS-CLACK: clacks are sharper (crest_factor ~7, millisecond attacks) and FASTER (~3-8/s), and carry no hiss bed. A chuff has a soft attack (~30 ms).
Against BOATS-STEAM: the same physics and the same signature. Text decides.
The chuff rate rises with speed; the periodicity persists, so the rate prior is given a wide deviation.

explanation: above group city trains probably best in TRAINS-ELECTRIC

discriminators: UCS defines this by ENVIRONMENT, not by source: 'overtly underground sounding trains; above ground city trains probably best in TRAINS-ELECTRIC.' The vehicle is identical to TRAINS-ELECTRIC. What differs is the TUNNEL.

So the only real handle is REVERBERATION — a long decay_time_seconds_60db (~1.8 s) and the heavy LF buildup of a confined concrete tube. That is an environment tell, not a source signature, which is why this is semantic_only rather than narrowable: a reverberant elevated station will fake it.
The whole electric-traction stack sits underneath (whine, clack, brake squeal), so no single morphology dominates — hence 'complex'.

explanation: cable cars trams and streetcars. A Tram is 'pulled' along a cable
so not self-powered. An electric tram would go to TRAINS-ELECTRIC.

discriminators: UCS draws a MECHANICAL line here, not an acoustic one: 'A Tram is pulled along a cable, so not self-powered. An electric tram would go to TRAINS-ELECTRIC.' Cable haulage is invisible to DSP.

The one physical consequence worth recording is a NEGATIVE: a cable car has no prime mover aboard, so there is no engine pulse train and no traction whine — total_harmonic_distortion stays low and onset_periodicity is modest. What is left is rail rumble, wheel squeal on tight curves, gripper clanks and a bell: a scene, not a morphology, hence 'complex'.
Against TRAINS-ELECTRIC: if a gliding traction whine is present (harmonicity high, pitch sliding), UCS's own rule sends it to TRAINS-ELECTRIC, not here.
